phrasalVerbs.definition: To make something happen, especially to cause changes in a situation.
examples: The new policy brought about significant improvements.
What brought about this sudden change in attitude?
Technology has brought about revolutionary changes in education.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To finish dealing with some work; to manage to deal with a difficult situation.
examples: I've got a lot of paperwork to get through today.
How did you get through such a difficult period?
She managed to get through the interview without any problems.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To produce a particular reaction; to be received in a certain way.
examples: The announcement went down well with investors.
His joke went down badly with the serious audience.
How did your proposal go down with the committee?

phrasalVerbs.definition: To succeed in doing something, but not in a very impressive way; to barely pass.
examples: He only scraped through the exam with the minimum pass mark.
The team scraped through to the finals.
She scraped through her driving test on the third attempt.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To be much more impressive or important than others; to be conspicuous.
examples: Her talent stood out from an early age.
What makes your application stand out from the rest?
One candidate really stood out during the interviews.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To become successful or popular very fast.
examples: The business really took off after they changed their marketing strategy.
Her career took off when she moved to London.
The app took off and gained millions of users within months.

fillInExercises.explanation: 'Bring about' means to cause something to happen.

fillInExercises.sentence: I have so much work to ___ through before the deadline.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Get through' means to finish dealing with work or difficulties.

fillInExercises.sentence: How did your presentation ___ with the clients?
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Go down' describes how something is received by others.

fillInExercises.sentence: She barely ___ through the qualification exam.
Her creative ideas ___ during the brainstorming session.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Stand out' means to be noticeably better than others.

fillInExercises.sentence: After the media coverage, sales really ___ off.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Take off' means to become suddenly successful.

fillInExercises.sentence: The new regulations ___ major changes in the industry.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Bring about' means to cause changes to happen.

fillInExercises.sentence: The new policy ___ down very well with employees.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Go down well' means to be received positively.

lines.speaker: Chen
lines.text: Your app really [took off|take off] after the last update!

explanationContent.whyCorrect: *take off* fits naturally in this line of the dialogue.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: *take off* — To become successful or popular very fast.
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Your app really took off after the last update!
explanationContent.commonMistake: Do not confuse this with other items from the lesson — here you need *take off* (*take off*).

wrongOptions.bring about: *bring about* (To make something happen, especially to cause changes in a situation) does not match the meaning needed in this line.
wrongOptions.get through: *get through* (To finish dealing with some work) does not match the meaning needed in this line.
wrongOptions.go down (well/badly): *go down (well/badly)* (To produce a particular reaction) does not match the meaning needed in this line.
wrongOptions.scrape through: *scrape through* (To succeed in doing something, but not in a very impressive way) does not match the meaning needed in this line.
wrongOptions.stand out: *stand out* (To be much more impressive or important than others) does not match the meaning needed in this line.

explanationContent.usefulTip: In conversation, *take off* is often used in informal spoken English.
